 Harif...In almost all of Iran’s universities, before the start of each semester, universities present a number of courses, and each course may be presented in a number of classes and by a number of professors. Then students must select some of these courses under some constraints, such as following a requisite and prerequisite courses chart, number of credits, etc. this leaves the students with many options to choose from. This was my B.Sc. project in which I tried to propose a practical approach to automate the process of course-selection, by suggesting course schedules based on student’s preferences.
Application Language(s): Persian
Programming Languages and Technologies: C#, Windows Presentation Foundation
Member(s): Taha Rostami
codes and more information (Available)
 NitPhoneBook...Babol Noshirvani University of Technology(BNUT) had a manual phonebook in a specified format. They asked me and my team to design an algorithm to print their phone data automatically in the same format as their manual phonebook. they also asked us to create a distributed application for creating, manipulating and printing phone books.
Application Language(s): English
Programming Languages and Technologies: C#, Windows Presentation Foundation, Microsoft SQL Server
Member(s): Taha Rostami, Behzad Pesaraklou, Amirhossein Majidi, Pourya Asgarianpour, Adnan Shahabi

title: Selcted Chess Games
content:
Selected Chess Games#I was a professional chess player throughout my teens. In the middle of the journey, I decided to focus on my studies and chose computer science as my new passion. So I left professional chess.
Fortunately, I had the chance to play with some of the best chess players in Iran. So I selected some of my games with them. these are some of the games that I’m most fond of, as they have both emotional and professional value to me.
You can find and see the selected games using links below:
 Kerim Aliev vs Taha Rostami, 2012 Taha Rostami vs Shahin Lorparizangeneh, 2012 Kaveh Ghaziolsharif vs Taha Rostami, 2012 Taha Rostami vs M.amin Tabatabaei, 2012 Ali Pilvaieh vs Taha Rostami, 2012 Seyed Khalil Mousavi vs Taha Rostami, 2012 Alireza Firouzja vs Taha Rostami, 2012 Amirreza Pourramezanali vs Taha Rostami, 2012 Farzad Bolourchifard vs Taha Rostami, 2012 Taha Rostami vs Amirsalar Javidfar, 2011 Taha Rostami vs Nima Javanbakht, 2011 Taha Rostami vs Rasoul Khosravi, 2011 Aryan Gholami vs Taha Rostami, 2011 Taha Rostami vs Sahoo Utkal Ranjan, 2010 Sharifi vs Taha Rostami, 2010  If you are interested to see more games, you can follow me on Lichess where I play chess yet as a hobby.
If you are interested to know more about my professional chess history, this is my profile in FIDE.
If you like to play chess with me, we can schedule (I provided my contact information in the Home Page) and play on lichess.
Learning and playing chess is easy, and many people can learn and play it well enough. Just some small details distinguish champions from others. If you are interested to learn chess, either as a hobby or as a professional, maybe I can help you. feel free to reach out.

title: Selcted Projects
content:
Selected Projects#Throughout these years, I wrote some programs and participated in some projects. I selected some of them and organized them into two categories Academic and Others and made some of them publicly available.
If you are interested to see more codes from me, you can follow me on hackerrank.
You can download my CV from the Home Page.
If you want to order software projects or have any kind of questions regarding programming, feel free to contact me using the information I provided on the Home Page.
If you have bad experiences with computer programming or computer science in the university or somewhere else, yet you like and hope to learn computer in-depth, maybe I can help you. before anything, it’s important to know almost everybody can learn computer. no need to be a genius, you just have to find the right path for you and work consistently.

title: Analysis of the FaRM article
content:
Removing Duplicates#We design an experiment two check duplicates instancses in the Codeflaws dataset. The experiment code as follows:
The experiment code...import numpy as np import pandas as pd import datetime import sys import warnings if not sys.warnoptions:warnings.simplefilter("ignore") cols = list(pd.read_csv(CODE_FLAWS_PATH, nrows =1)) d1=datetime.datetime.now() X=pd.read_csv(CODE_FLAWS_PATH,header=0,dtype=np.float16, usecols =[i for i in cols if i != 'projectID' and i!='mutantID']) #split dataset into two disjoint fault-rev and not-fault-rev mutants fault_rev=X[X['coupling-weight']==1] not_fault_rev=X[X['coupling-weight']!=1] print(f"fault rev: {len(fault_rev)}, not fault rev: {len(not_fault_rev)}, total:{len(fault_rev)+len(not_fault_rev)}") lst=list(not_fault_rev.columns) lst.remove('coupling-weight') fault_rev.drop_duplicates(keep='first',subset=lst,inplace=True) not_fault_rev.drop_duplicates(keep='first',subset=lst,inplace=True) print(f"fault rev: {len(fault_rev)}, not fault rev: {len(not_fault_rev)}, total:{len(fault_rev)+len(not_fault_rev)}") final=pd.concat([fault_rev,not_fault_rev]) print(f"fault rev: {len(final[final['coupling-weight']==1])}, not fault rev: {len(final[final['coupling-weight']!=1])}, total:{len(final)}") print(datetime.datetime.now()-d1) after execution of the above experiment the results as follows:
   Description number of fault-rev number of not-fault-rev total number     before begining the experiment 44,221 1,711,810 1,756,031   after rmv. duplicate items seperately for fault_rev and not_fault_rev datasets 35,278 1,306,872 1,342,150   after above work, concat two seperate datasets and rmv. duplicates again 35,278 1,303,824 1,339,102    We also perform this experiment when we use just numberical features of the dataset:
   Description number of fault-rev number of not-fault-rev total number     before begining the experiment 44,221 1,711,810 1,756,031   after rmv. duplicate items seperately for fault_rev and not_fault_rev datasets 8,787 179,015 187,802   after above work, concat two seperate datasets and rmv. duplicates again 8,787 172,376 181,163    From the above results we know:
  416,929 mutants in the dataset are exactly equivalents (23.7% from the total mutants).
  For the 3,048‬ not-fault revealing mutants, exists at least one fault-revealing mutants with the excatly same embedding vector (i.e. for 8.6% of the fault-rev mutants the same instance exists with the label of not-fault-rev).
  From the Observation 2 we know there exists some instances like below:
   Feature1 Feature2 … FeatureX target     V1 V2 … VX fault-rev   V1 V2 … VX not-fault-rev    We argue that there are probably many instances that are very, very close to each other.
We also claim for an arbitrary fault-rev mutant with the embedding vector V, we can generate unlimited number of vector close to it which they are not fault revaling mutants.
So I believe the dataset and real data distribution like below:
For clearing our understanding about the correctness of the above argument, I calculated euclidean distance between fault-rev and fault-rev mutants after duplicate mutants removal. But yet I could not to calculate pairwise distances for other possibilites (i.e. fault-rev&not-fault-rev, not-fault-rev&not-fault-rev and all_mutants&all_mutants).
According to the above argument, We think the best approach we can follow is using clustering for calssification!
But some challenges exists:
 It seems density based and transductive clustering approaches like DBScan best suite for our purpose but theie implementaion doesn’t support predict function for new data.  What distance measures must we use? All of our features are between [0,1] and our dataset dimension is high so It seems Euclidean distance doesn’t suite.

title: Introduction to Code-based Mutation Testing
content:
preface#A piece of software may have bugs and the presence of bugs may cause problems. So Software Industry uses some methods to reduce the risk of existence of bugs. One of this method is Software Testing which is a process of evaluating a software system to ensure its correct and optimal behavior or to find possible errors in it. One of the most effective techniques in Software Testing is Mutation Testing.
Testing can apply to different software engineering artifacts, but now we focus on Testing software program codes. In the next sections, we introduce you with code-based mutation testing (i.e. testing software codes using mutation testing) and the concepts related to it.
Mutation Testing Overview#We design an example to explain the mutation testing step by step. Assume we ask a programmer to write a method name Compare with the following behavior:
\(Compare(x,y)= \begin{cases}\space\space\space1 &\text{if } x>y \\\space\space\space0 &\text{if } x=y \\-1 &\text{if } x<y\end{cases}\)Now Assume a programmer write a program like below, for this purpose:
Compare(x, y)1. const c=12. var res=c3. if x>=y:4. res=-1/res5. if x==y:6. res=res+c7. print(res)But maybe the programmer made a mistake in writing the program code. So to enusring about bug freeness of the program, we ask a test desginer to design test suite for testing the program.
Now Assume a test designer desgin the following as the test suite:
    Test input Test Oracle     Test case 1 (x:0 , y:0) expected std. output: 0   Test case 2 (x:8 , y:8) expected std. output: 0    Now we have both source code and test suite for it. So we can execute the program with respect to each test case of the test suite. If program behavior(i.e. it’s output) is expected with respect to each test case, it’s fine. But if the program behave as not expected this means we find a bug in the program.
If we excute the above program with respect to it’s test suite, the program behave as excpected in all cases. In this case, two cases are possible:
 the program is bug free. the program may have a bug but because of our poor test suite, we did not find it yet!  The question 2 implies that Are Our test suite is strong enough to reveal potential bugs of the program?
Mutation Testing is one the approach we can use for answering the question 2, which we will talk about now.

title: Reimplementation of the FaRM article
content:
Some Notes About FaRM article#Fault Revealing Mutant Selection Prblem Definition#Thierry Titcheu Chekam et. al. defined a fault revealing mutant selection problem as:
Given: A set of mutants M for program P.
Problem: Subset selection. Select a subset of mutants, S ∈ M, such that F (S) = F (M) and (∀m ∈ S), (F (S − {m}) = F (M)).
S represents a subset of M; F (X) represents the number of faults in P that are revealed by the test suites that kill all the mutants of the set X. In practice, the challenge is to approximate well S, statically and prior to any test execution, by finding a relatively good trade-off between the number of selected mutants (to minimise) and the number of faults revealed by their killing (to maximize).
Their Implementaion#For this purpose, they used C programs prepered in the Codeflaws dataset. Codeflaws tree structure as follows:
 Number of problems defined for online programming competition which we called each problem as project (problem = project). Number of problem might solved with different programmers, this means we have one or more implementations for each problem. we called each of this program as implementaion (program = implementaion). for each implementaion we have two versions, one is sumbited program whcih rejected because of existence of bug(s) in it. another is submited program which accepted because of the test suite did not find any bug in it. In the absence of the oracles for the programs, they use accepted one behavior as expected behavior and all of their mutation performed on the rejected one.  They produce Mutants for each C programs using Mart. Mart get a C program, and perform mutation on the llvm file of the C program. In particular they said that all of their analysis are based on llvm codes (not C codes).
Codeflaws also contains some test cases for each problem, but as they might not be strong enough, they also produce strong test inputs for each of the problem using KLEE.
They remove equivalent mutants that identified by the TCE.
After that, they execute mutation analysis process and using the matrix execution calculate utility for each of the mutants. they consider utility of the mutant as #number of test cases that kill the mutant and reveal faults / #number of test cases that kill the mutants. If mutant did not kill by any test cases they consider it as equivalent mutant and set it’s utility as -1. They also remove from the dataset, trivial mutants which are mutants that many test cases can kill them.
For reminder of the mutants, they extract feature values. and then made the dataset available.
Note their experiment is performed on the mention dataset (without any change). According our communication with them, they verified that the following pseudocode is what they perform in experiment 1 of their work:
01. dataset <- load Codeflaws dataset02. dataset['coupling-weight'] <- 'fault-rev' if dataset['coupling-weight']=1 else 'not-fault-rev'03. parts <- randomly splitting the dataset into 10 parts such that all implementations of the same problem appear exactly in one part04. for (each part in parts)05. testx,y <- part06. Trainx,y <- parts \ part07. trainx,y <- Oversample(trainx,y)08. model <-StochasticGradientBoostingClassifier(depth=5,tree=1000).FitModel(trainx,y)09. preds <- model.PredProb(testx,y).sort()10. select 05% top from preds and then report precision, recall, f111. select 10% top from preds and then report precision, recall, f112. select 20% top from preds and then report precision, recall, f113. report AUCThey are consider confusin matrix as follows:
    Predicted fault-rev Predicted not fault-rev     Actually fault-rev TP FN   Actually not fault-rev FP TN    according to the above table we know:
\(\boxed{\text{Precision }=\frac {number {\space} of {\space}fault-rev {\space}mutants{\space} which{\space} predicted{\space} as {\space} fault-rev} {number {\space} of {\space}mutants{\space} predicted{\space} as{\space} fault-rev}}\)\(\boxed{\text{Recall }=\frac {number {\space} of {\space}fault-rev {\space}mutants{\space} which{\space} predicted{\space} as {\space} fault-rev} {number {\space} of {\space}actual{\space} fault-rev{\space} mutants}}\)They report their results of the experiment 1 as follows:
   AUC:62.04% Precision Recall F1     5% top mutants 5.7% 12.8% 7.8%   10% to mutants 4.9% 22% 8%   20% to mutants 3.9% 35.1% 7%    NOTE
In this application recall is seem more appealing that precision.Dataset Tree Structure# ds└── project-i└── implementaion-j└── line-k└── mutant-M1Dataset Tree Structure in more details... ds├── project-1│ ├── implementaion-1│ │ ├── line-0│ │ │ ├── mutant-1│ │ │ ├── mutant-2│ │ │ ...│ │ │ └── mutant-M1│ │ ││ │ ├── line-1│ │ │ ├── mutant-M1+1│ │ │ ├── reimplementaion...import numpy as np import pandas as pd import os from sklearn.model_selection import GroupKFold from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingClassifier from sklearn import metrics from imblearn.over_sampling import RandomOverSampler import Config.config as config with open(config.original_codeflaws_ds_provided_by_article_filename,"r")\ as f: columns = f.readline().strip('\n').split(',') prj_names=list(set(map(lambda impl_name: impl_name[:impl_name.find('-bug')] ,os.listdir(config.original_codeflaws_matrices_dir_provided_bt_article_filename)))) =[i for i in columns if i!='mutantID'], converters= {'projectID':lambda impl_name:prj_names_encode_value_dict[impl_name[:impl_name.find('-bug')]]}) y = X['coupling-weight'].apply(lambda w:1 if w==1 group_kfold.split(X, y, groups): np.random.shuffle(train_index) X_train, X_test = X.loc[train_index], X.loc[test_index] y_train, y_test = y.loc[train_index], y.loc[test_index] try: oversample = RandomOverSampler(sampling_strategy=1) X_train_resample, y_train_resample = oversample.fit_resample(X_train, y_train) clf = GradientBoostingClassifier(n_estimators=1000, learning_rate=1.0,subsample=0.9,max_depth=5)\ .fit(X_train_resample, y_train_resample) y_preds_proba=clf.predict_proba(X_test)[:,1] auc=metrics.roc_auc_score(y_test,y_preds_proba) auc_s.append(auc) print(auc) except Exception as e: print("An exception occurred: ", e) os.system('clear' if os.name =='posix' else 'cls') print(auc_s) print(f" min: {np.min(auc_s)}, mean: {np.mean(auc_s)}, max: {np.max(auc_s)}, stdev: {np.std(auc_s)}")

Notes About SMOTE Oversampling#Procedure#SMOTE first selects a minority class instance a at random and finds its k nearest minority class neighbors. The synthetic instance is then created by choosing one of the k nearest neighbors b at random and connecting a and b to form a line segment in the feature space. The synthetic instances are generated as a convex combination of the two chosen instances a and b.
How to use it in a effective manner#This procedure can be used to create as many synthetic examples for the minority class as are required. As described in the paper, it suggests first using random undersampling to trim the number of examples in the majority class, then use SMOTE to oversample the minority class to balance the class distribution.
Why can be a effective technique#The approach is effective because new synthetic examples from the minority class are created that are plausible, that is, are relatively close in feature space to existing examples from the minority class.
When can not be effective#The approach is effective because new synthetic examples from the minority class are created that are plausible, that is, are relatively close in feature space to existing examples from the minority class.
Random Oversampling#Works#Random oversampling involves randomly duplicating examples from the minority class and adding them to the training dataset.
Examples from the training dataset are selected randomly with replacement. This means that examples from the minority class can be chosen and added to the new “more balanced” training dataset multiple times; they are selected from the original training dataset, added to the new training dataset, and then returned or “replaced” in the original dataset, allowing them to be selected again.
When can be effective#This technique can be effective for those machine learning algorithms that are affected by a skewed distribution and where multiple duplicate examples for a given class can influence the fit of the model. This might include algorithms that iteratively learn coefficients, like artificial neural networks that use stochastic gradient descent. It can also affect models that seek good splits of the data, such as support vector machines and decision trees.
When can not be effective and How check it#It might be useful to tune the target class distribution. In some cases, seeking a balanced distribution for a severely imbalanced dataset can cause affected algorithms to overfit the minority class, leading to increased generalization error. The effect can be better performance on the training dataset, but worse performance on the holdout or test dataset.
As such, to gain insight into the impact of the method, it is a good idea to monitor the performance on both train and test datasets after oversampling and compare the results to the same algorithm on the original dataset.
The increase in the number of examples for the minority class, especially if the class skew was severe, can also result in a marked increase in the computational cost when fitting the model, especially considering the model is seeing the same examples in the training dataset again and again.

title: machine learning notes 2
content:
Linear dimensionalilty reduction algorithms strengths and weaknesses#A number of supervised and unsupervised linear dimensionality reduction frameworks have been designed, such as Principal Component Analysis (PCA), Independent Component Analysis, Linear Discriminant Analysis, and others. These algorithms define specific rubrics to choose an “interesting” linear projection of the data. These methods can be powerful, but often miss important non-linear structure in the data.
Statistics for Filter-Based Feature Selection Methods#Numerical Input, Categorical Output#  ANOVA correlation coefficient (linear).
  Kendall’s rank coefficient (nonlinear).
  Kendall does assume that the categorical variable is ordinal.
Categorical Input, Categorical Output#  Chi-Squared test (contingency tables).
  Mutual Information.
  In fact, mutual information is a powerful method that may prove useful for both categorical and numerical data, e.g. it is agnostic to the data types.
Selection Method#  Select the top k variables: SelectKBest
  Select the top percentile variables: SelectPercentile
  Transform Variables#Consider transforming the variables in order to access different statistical methods.
For example, you can transform a categorical variable to ordinal, even if it is not, and see if any interesting results come out.
You can also make a numerical variable discrete (e.g. bins); try categorical-based measures.
Some statistical measures assume properties of the variables, such as Pearson’s that assumes a Gaussian probability distribution to the observations and a linear relationship. You can transform the data to meet the expectations of the test and try the test regardless of the expectations and compare results.
Some notes about LDA#LDA makes some simplifying assumptions about your data:
  That your data is Gaussian, that each variable is is shaped like a bell curve when plotted.
  That each attribute has the same variance, that values of each variable vary around the mean by the same amount on average.
  How to Prepare Data for LDA#  Classification Problems. This might go without saying, but LDA is intended for classification problems where the output variable is categorical. LDA supports both binary and multi-class classification.
  Gaussian Distribution. The standard implementation of the model assumes a Gaussian distribution of the input variables. Consider reviewing the univariate distributions of each attribute and using transforms to make them more Gaussian-looking (e.g. log and root for exponential distributions and Box-Cox for skewed distributions).
  Remove Outliers. Consider removing outliers from your data. These can skew the basic statistics used to separate classes in LDA such the mean and the standard deviation.
  Same Variance. LDA assumes that each input variable has the same variance. It is almost always a good idea to standardize your data before using LDA so that it has a mean of 0 and a standard deviation of 1.
